# Design Principles (U.S. Web Design System)

Five core design principles published by USWDS, the U.S. federal government's shared design system, framed as an evaluative checklist for federal digital services under the 21st Century Integrated Digital Experience Act. The first source in this wiki that isn't a chapter of *The Design of Sites*.

## Key points

- **Start with Real User Needs** — product decisions driven by ongoing research with real people, not assumptions. Mostly reconfirms what's already covered in [[know-your-customers]] and [[customer-research-methods]]; not separately expanded here.
- **Earn Trust** — trust is never assumed, only earned and re-earned: clear identity signals, protecting sensitive data, system reliability, and honest, plain-language communication. Promoted to its own page, [[trust-and-credibility]], since no existing page treated trust as a first-class design principle in its own right.
- **Embrace Accessibility** — folded into [[accessibility]], adding named standards (WCAG 2.1, Revised Section 508), keyboard/screen-reader navigability, and multilingual content — dimensions that page didn't have yet, which previously covered only color-vision deficiency and motor-control target sizing. The WCAG/Section 508 material was later split out into its own page, [[wcag]], once two dedicated Wikipedia sources made that standard's structure substantial enough to outgrow a single paragraph on [[accessibility]] — this source's Section 508 contrast point lives there now.
- **Promote Continuity** — folded into [[interface-design-principles]]'s "Be consistent throughout" principle, extending it with cross-device, cross-browser, and cross-session consistency.
- **Listen** — folded into [[web-site-development-process]]'s Maintenance phase, extending its existing metrics/server-log content with bug-reporting channels, dedicated analytics tooling, and publishing metrics back to stakeholders.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is now saved at `raw/safe/Design principles _ U.S. Web Design System (USWDS).pdf`, closing the offline-re-verification gap flagged when this source was first ingested. The citing pages' `sources:` fields have been updated to reference it alongside the live URL. Reading the actual raw text (rather than the earlier AI-summarized fetch) confirmed everything in the Key Points above and surfaced a few additional verified specifics, since folded into [[trust-and-credibility]]: "Earn Trust" also covers identifying and fixing link rot and writing for skimming/scanning rather than dense paragraphs; "Promote Continuity" also covers giving proper notice and forwarding when content is moved or removed. None of these contradict what's already written — they're additions, not corrections. One remaining unfolded specific: "Earn Trust" also covers publishing open code/data, which doesn't yet have a clear home on any existing page.

The page displays no explicit copyright/license statement, but as a work of the U.S. federal government it is presumptively public domain under 17 U.S.C. § 105.

# Advanced E-Commerce

Pattern Group G of *The Design of Sites* — seven patterns (G1-G7) building on the basic e-commerce checkout flow ([[vanduyne-2006-basic-e-commerce|Pattern Group F]]) with optional features for promoting products, personalizing the shopping experience, and supporting customers after the sale. Documents all seven in full six-part format, per [[web-design-patterns]].

## Key points

- [[featured-products]] (G1) — curated, editorialized product lists (Best-Sellers, Editors' Picks, On Sale) for browsing-minded customers who don't arrive with a specific product in mind.
- [[cross-selling-and-up-selling]] (G2) — recommending accessories or upgrades related to a customer's current choice, kept visually secondary to the main product and repeated at checkout in case it was missed.
- [[personalized-recommendations]] (G3) — recommendations built on deliberate signals (ratings, a short preference interview) rather than noisy inferred browsing data, with explicit attention to privacy disclosure.
- [[recommendation-community]] (G4) — customer-written reviews: a two-step write/preview funnel, use policies and filtering, and meta-ratings (Amazon's "helpful?" votes, eBay's buyer/seller feedback score) to surface trustworthy reviews.
- [[multiple-destinations]] (G5) — shipping different items in one order to different addresses, with a destination-grouped order summary.
- [[gift-giving]] (G6) — price-hiding packing slips, per-item gift notes and wrap pricing, and a gift-finder recommendation flow.
- [[order-tracking-and-history]] (G7) — self-service order history, modification, and shipment tracking, replacing phone-based customer service for routine status questions.

## Notes for the wiki

Four of the seven patterns (G1-G4) are explicitly described as alternative, *non-exclusive* ways to provide product recommendations — a site can use any or all simultaneously — so each got its own page rather than being merged, unlike e.g. [[content-organization-schemes]] (B3-B7), which the book presents as five mutually-exclusive answers to one decision.

**Entities:** [[walmart]] and [[lands-end|Lands' End]] both cross the 2+-source entity threshold here — each had previously appeared only as a bare, unexplained name (Walmart in Pattern Group B's list of examples; Lands' End as an uncaptioned screenshot in Pattern Group I) and this chapter gives each real depth (Walmart: [[multiple-destinations]] and [[gift-giving]]; Lands' End: the preference-interview technique in [[personalized-recommendations]]). [[amazon]], [[ebay]], and [[yahoo|Yahoo!]] all gained substantial new material, folded into their existing pages rather than duplicated.

Stayed below the entity threshold despite a second mention: **Martha Stewart's site** and **Barnes & Noble** — both now appear in two sources, but neither mention (here or previously) goes beyond a single bare or lightly-captioned screenshot, the same standard that's kept CNN and Epicurious below threshold in earlier sessions. **Netflix** appears twice in this chapter alone (cross-sell floating window in G2, a star-rating system in G3) but that's still one source no matter how many times it's used within it, so it stays inline regardless of depth. 1-800-Flowers, Brooks Brothers, Ann Taylor, The Knot, CD Universe, RedEnvelope, TiVo, Nordstrom, and Shutterfly are single-source incidental examples.

**Media:** 40 screenshots/figures extracted from the PDF (via `pypdf`, since no PDF-render tool like `pdftoppm`/`pdfimages` is available in this environment) and saved to `raw/media/vanduyne-2006-advanced-e-commerce/`, named by figure number (e.g. `G1.1.png`, `G3.4a.png`). A few pages yielded more raster chunks than the text's figure count implied (G6.2/G6.2-Continued, G7.2) — inspected each one directly and confirmed they're genuinely separate screenshots from the same multi-step flow, not duplicate tiles, and named them with sequential letter suffixes (`G6.2a`-`G6.2d`, `G7.2a`-`G7.2b`) accordingly. The chapter's purely schematic "Solution" diagrams (G1.5, G2.7, G3.5, G4.6, G5.5, G6.4, G7.4) didn't extract as raster images — likely vector graphics in the original PDF — so no files exist for those.

**Excluded** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): Table G1.1/G1.2's specific commonly-asked-question/pattern-cross-reference grids and Table G7.1/G7.2's order-status/modification-routing grids — summarized in prose in the relevant concept pages rather than reproduced as tables, consistent with this wiki's general preference for prose over reproduced book tables; G4's footnoted Pew Internet & American Life Project citation detail beyond the one statistic actually used; AJAX as an implementation detail (G3's ratings-without-reload mention) per the code-implementation exclusion. Cross-references to patterns in not-yet-ingested groups (A, D, E, H, K) were kept as plain text rather than forced links — Group F has since been ingested as [[vanduyne-2006-basic-e-commerce]] (pages: [[quick-flow-checkout]], [[shopping-cart]], [[clean-product-details]], [[easy-returns]]). This chapter leaned especially heavily on Group F and Group H (PROCESS FUNNEL — already covered by [[process-funnel]] from Ch. 2 — plus SIGN-IN/NEW ACCOUNT, GUEST ACCOUNT, FLOATING WINDOW, PREDICTIVE INPUT, DRILL-DOWN OPTIONS), reinforcing both as priorities for future ingestion.

# The Design of Sites: Pattern Group F — Basic E-Commerce

Pattern Group F from *The Design of Sites* (van Duyne, Landay, and Hong, 2006). Covers the nine core patterns for a complete basic e-commerce checkout experience, from product detail pages through post-purchase confirmation and easy returns.

**Patterns documented:**

- **F1 QUICK-FLOW CHECKOUT** — a distraction-free checkout funnel that answers deal-breaker questions upfront, allows guest checkout, builds trust through security signals, and guides customers through a four-step sequence; see [[quick-flow-checkout]]
- **F2 CLEAN PRODUCT DETAILS** — a standardized product page template with above-fold priority elements (thumbnail, description, price, options, Add to Cart) and below-fold secondary content (full description, reviews, related products); see [[clean-product-details]]
- **F3 SHOPPING CART** — the holding area for items before checkout, with persistent storage, sufficient item detail, wish lists, shipping estimates, and secondary cross-selling; see [[shopping-cart]]
- **F4 QUICK ADDRESS SELECTION** — address book for returning customers, single-column minimized form for new addresses; see [[quick-flow-checkout]]
- **F5 QUICK SHIPPING METHOD SELECTION** — transparent cost and delivery time shown simultaneously, live cost update when method changes; see [[quick-flow-checkout]]
- **F6 PAYMENT METHOD** — security signals at payment entry, stored card references by last four digits, promotion/gift-certificate redemption; see [[quick-flow-checkout]]
- **F7 ORDER SUMMARY** — final pre-submission review with all information shown and editable per section, high-visibility "Place Order" button above and below fold; see [[quick-flow-checkout]]
- **F8 ORDER CONFIRMATION AND THANK-YOU** — post-purchase confirmation page with order number, full summary, email receipt, printable view, and optional secondary cross-sell; see [[quick-flow-checkout]]
- **F9 EASY RETURNS** — return policy links on every product and checkout page, plus a step-by-step online return funnel with trackable labels; see [[easy-returns]]

**Sites cited as examples:** Amazon, Half.com, Nordstrom, Snapfish, Barnes & Noble, Room & Board, Netflix, Cooking.com, babyGap, Buy.com, United Airlines.

# Creating a Navigation Framework

Pattern Group B of *The Design of Sites* — nine patterns (B1-B9) for letting customers move through a site in whatever way suits how they arrived and what they're after, since customers rarely all enter at the homepage or want the same thing.

## Key points

- [[multiple-ways-to-navigate]] (B1) — the intention/impulse motivator framework and the five navigation styles (search, browse, next-step, relate, promote) that serve them, plus where each belongs on the page.
- [[browsable-content]] (B2) — organizing content for browsing rather than searching, the information-scent concept (coined at Xerox PARC), and finding-your-way-back cues.
- [[content-organization-schemes]] (B3-B7, combined into one page) — hierarchical, task-based, alphabetical, chronological, and popularity-based organization, presented in the book as a menu of interchangeable/combinable answers to one question rather than five separate problems.
- [[category-pages]] (B8) — keeping a site's distinct sections recognizably part of one whole, via consistent layout and a clear "you've arrived" signal.
- SITE ACCESSIBILITY (B9) — folded into the existing [[accessibility]] page rather than given its own (see Notes below).

## Notes for the wiki

This chapter resolves two long-flagged documentation gaps: MULTIPLE WAYS TO NAVIGATE (B1) and BROWSABLE CONTENT (B2) had been referenced, unlinked, from several earlier-ingested chapters and concept pages without ever being explained — both now have full pages.

B9 (SITE ACCESSIBILITY) was **not** given its own page, unlike the other eight patterns in this chapter. By the time this chapter was ingested, [[accessibility]] already covered POUR, WCAG, WAI-ARIA, color-vision deficiency, motor control, and screen-reader/assistive-technology support in depth from four prior Wikipedia/USWDS ingestions — most of B9's content (Section 508, alt text, color contrast, keyboard navigation) simply re-confirmed what was already there. The genuinely new material was folded into [[accessibility]] instead: the curb-cut-effect framing for the business case, a concrete green-unvisited/red-visited link-color example, a 2005 study (Mankoff, Fait, and Tran) on the relative effectiveness of different accessibility-testing methods, and a brief note connecting style-sheet-based content/presentation separation to accessibility specifically.

Card sorting's role in [[content-organization-schemes|hierarchical organization]] (B3) is paired here with two testing techniques not previously in this wiki — category identification and category description — both folded into [[card-sorting]] rather than left only on the organization-schemes page, since they're squarely card-sorting-adjacent testing methods. Both are described more fully in a pattern this chapter references but doesn't itself contain, FAMILIAR LANGUAGE (K11), not yet ingested.

**Excluded:** the chapter's specific accessibility-tool recommendations (WebXACT/Bobby, the Colorblind Web Page Filter, Vischeck, Fangs) as named, mostly-defunct circa-2001-2006 software products — implementation tooling, not design principle, consistent with how other defunct named tools (the Web Metrics Tool Suite, UsableNet Liftmachine) were excluded from the usability-engineering ingestion; the W3C's older "ten quick tips" checklist, since it's a simplified precursor to the POUR/WCAG framework already documented in depth via [[wcag]]; HTML/CSS specifics (`<alt>`, `<title>`, `<longdesc>` attribute syntax) per `purpose.md`'s implementation-code exclusion; and the mobile-device-specific accessibility tips (MOBILE SCREEN SIZING, MOBILE INPUT CONTROLS), since Pattern Group M (the Mobile Web) hasn't been ingested yet and these patterns aren't explained anywhere else in this wiki to link against.

No new entities. Wal-Mart, Sun.com, Salesforce.com, FindLaw, Billboard, Flickr, the "Lycos 50 Daily Report," Martha Stewart's site, REI.com, and the Weather Channel are single-source incidental examples; Yahoo! and Amazon each get another incidental confirming example here (hierarchical directory, intention/impulse navigation respectively) but no new substance beyond what their existing entity pages already say.

# Creating a Powerful Homepage

Pattern Group C of *The Design of Sites* — the first of the book's lettered pattern groups ingested into this wiki. Documents two patterns in full six-part format (background/problem/forces/solution/other-patterns, per [[web-design-patterns]]): [[homepage-portal]] (C1) and [[up-front-value-proposition]] (C2).

## Key points

- [[homepage-portal]] (C1) frames the homepage as the site's single highest-leverage page — seen far more often than any other — and lays out the competing demands (brand, look-and-feel, content, personalization, navigation clarity, download speed) it has to satisfy at once within the limited space above the fold.
- A recurring rule of thumb: dedicate roughly 95% of above-the-fold space/links to the audience subgroup that makes up 95% of visitors, with the remaining 5% (often reached via a role-selection page) for everyone else.
- [[up-front-value-proposition]] (C2) gives a concrete, four-step, customer-tested process (team brainstorm → stakeholder shortlist → customer importance/uniqueness rating → customer pick) for arriving at the one-sentence promise that anchors a homepage — directly extending the "decide on the Web site's value proposition" step named but not detailed back in [[web-site-development-process]]'s Discovery phase.
- Both patterns lean heavily on patterns from other groups already touched on inline elsewhere in this wiki — [[search-action-module]], grid layout, page templates, clear first reads, [[multiple-ways-to-navigate]] — reinforcing the "pattern language" idea from [[web-design-patterns]] that patterns complete and reference each other rather than standing alone.

## Notes for the wiki

No new entities. The chapter's screenshot examples (MSNBC, My Yahoo!, Epicurious, Snapfish, TechBargains, CultureFinder, Ciena) are each single, incidental, illustrative mentions — below the 2+ source threshold — except [[yahoo]] (the My Yahoo! personalization screenshot), which already has an entity page from prior sources; this is simply a fourth incidental mention, not added as new substance to that page. David Ogilvy / *Ogilvy on Advertising* is a single citation, not promoted to an entity.

# Customer-Centered Web Design: More Than a Good Idea

Chapter 1 of *The Design of Sites* — the book's introductory chapter, making the case for [[customer-centered-design]] before the rest of the book gets into specific principles, processes, and patterns.

## Key points

- Frames the Web's design history as four generations: "build it and they will come" (first), "advertise it and they will come" (second, the dot-com ad-spending era), customer-centered design (third, the book's own contribution), and a fourth generation shaped by AJAX/Web 2.0/mobile, which raises both the ceiling and the difficulty of building good interfaces.
- Defines a **customer-centered Web site** as one that scores well on five factors: content, ease of use, performance, brand value/trustworthiness, and satisfaction — and explains the deliberate choice of *customer* over *user* (broader scope: administrators, partners, managers, not just end-users; and an implicit nod to taking the relationship more seriously than the term "user" suggests).
- Makes a business case with concrete numbers: an unnamed IBM redesign that simplified navigation and cut reliance on search/help, followed a week later by a 400% jump in online sales; and the authors' own research finding that a 5% increase in customer satisfaction can produce a 25%+ increase in revenue, via two mechanisms (easier to find things → more repeat visits; fewer support contacts and returns → lower costs).
- Contrasts [[customer-centered-design]] against three competing approaches it explicitly argues against — company-centered, technology-centered, and designer-centered design — and against the related-but-narrower user-centered design it builds on; covered in [[design-centering-styles]].
- Rebuts nine common objections to investing in customer-centered design (e.g. "good design is just common sense," "experts only," "we'll fix the interface right before launch," "it costs too much," "guidelines are enough," "QA covers this"); covered in [[myths-of-customer-centered-design]].
- Frames the rest of the book around three components — **principles** (Ch. 3–4: [[know-your-customers]], [[iterative-design]]), **processes** (Ch. 5: [[web-site-development-process]]), and **patterns** (the pattern groups A–M covered in later chapters) — and explains how they interact: principles guide trade-off decisions between process activities and between competing pattern-based solutions.

## Notes for the wiki

No new entities crossed the 2+ source threshold from *this* chapter alone, but several companies it names incidentally (Yahoo!, Amazon.com, Google, eBay) had already been named incidentally in Chapters 2 and/or 5 — so across the corpus so far, each now has 2–3 source mentions with enough cumulative substance (Yahoo!'s dedicated "Providing Tangible Value" sidebar in this chapter in particular) to justify minimal entity pages: [[yahoo]], [[amazon]], [[google]], [[ebay]]. Flickr, CNN, MSN, and General Motors remain single-source incidental mentions and stay inline text. IBM's redesign/sales-jump business case here was also single-source at the time, but later picked up a second, substantive mention in Pattern Group J (search-term synonym handling) — that second mention pushed it over the threshold, and [[ibm]] now has a minimal entity page drawing on this chapter as one of its two sources. Beyer and Holtzblatt's *Contextual Design* (cited as the source of the book's "customer" terminology) is a single incidental citation, not promoted to an entity page.

# Designing Effective Page Layouts

Pattern Group I of *The Design of Sites* — six patterns (I1-I6) for giving a Web page a clear, predictable structure. Documents all six in full six-part format, per [[web-design-patterns]].

## Key points

- [[grid-layout]] (I1) — organizing a page's competing elements into a consistent row/column structure that customers can learn to predict.
- [[above-the-fold]] (I2) — what must be visible without scrolling, and why the Web's variable screen sizes make this a harder problem than it was in print.
- [[clear-first-reads]] (I3) — finally gives full-pattern depth to "first read," a term this wiki had previously only referenced in passing (in [[homepage-portal]]) and explicitly flagged as a documentation gap in the 2026-06-24 lint pass.
- [[expanding-and-fixed-screen-width]] (I4 + I5) — combined into one page since the book always presents these two as a direct contrast with each other.
- [[consistent-sidebars-of-related-content]] (I6) — where and how to place links to related content so visitors actually notice them.

## Notes for the wiki

PAGE TEMPLATE (D1) is referenced by every single pattern in this chapter — it's described as the umbrella pattern all six of I1-I6 contribute to — but it's never itself explained here; it lives in the not-yet-ingested Pattern Group D. This is now a stronger documentation gap than SITE BRANDING (E1) / PRIVACY POLICY (E4), flagged in earlier sessions, given how central it evidently is; prioritize Pattern Group D next if a choice has to be made.

No new entities. CNN.com appears here as a screenshot example (I6, sidebars) — its third incidental mention across ingested sources (after the Ch. 1/Ch. 2 "durability" lists) — but it's still just a name-drop or screenshot each time, never a substantive paragraph the way Yahoo! got in Chapter 1, so it stays below the entity threshold for now; worth reconsidering if a future source adds real depth. Lands' End, Williams-Sonoma, Outpost.com, iWon, CNET News.com, IFILM, Beliefnet, the EPA, and Wichita State University (the placement-expectation research cited in both [[grid-layout]] and [[above-the-fold]]) are all single-source incidental examples.

Heavy implementation content — HTML table-width syntax and style-sheet code for I4/I5, and a table-border debugging trick for I1 — was excluded per `purpose.md`'s "specific code implementation" rule; only the underlying design rationale was kept.

# Processes for Developing Customer-Centered Sites

Chapter 5 of *The Design of Sites*. Synthesizes the patterns, principles, and techniques from Chapters 2–4 into a single end-to-end project process — [[web-site-development-process]] — covering seven phases from initial discovery through ongoing maintenance.

## Key points

- The chapter frames its seven-phase process as a deliberately non-linear, [[iterative-design|iterative]] structure: teams jump backward to earlier phases whenever evaluation reveals a problem, and doing so is framed as time/money saved, not a setback.
- The first four phases (Discovery, Exploration, Refinement, Production) are "the design process" — each moves from general to increasingly specific, each itself runs a tight design-prototype-evaluate loop, and each ends in a client checkpoint with concrete deliverables. The last three (Implementation, Launch, Maintenance) cover building, shipping, and sustaining the site.
- Discovery produces three documents — customer analysis, business analysis, and a specification — that anchor every later decision; the chapter calls *not writing a specification document* "the biggest risk you can take," and warns against two common failure modes: skipping Discovery outright, and **gold-plating** (perfecting flashy-but-low-value features instead of shipping core ones in stages).
- Exploration and Refinement reuse the [[rapid-prototyping]] artifacts from Chapter 4 (site maps, storyboards, schematics) at increasing fidelity, each tested with customers via [[usability-evaluation-methods|evaluation]] before the client commits to one direction.
- Production, Implementation, Launch, and Maintenance get progressively more technical and operational — design/technical-spec documents, page templates, QA checklists, staged rollouts, and the ongoing decision of when a site needs a minor revision versus a complete redesign.

## Notes for the wiki

No new entities met the 2+ source threshold. Most named design patterns in this chapter (UP-FRONT VALUE PROPOSITION, SITE BRANDING, PERSONALIZED CONTENT, HOMEPAGE PORTAL, PRIVACY POLICY, CLEAR FIRST READS, GRID LAYOUT, OBVIOUS LINKS, PAGE TEMPLATES, ABOVE THE FOLD, EXPANDING SCREEN WIDTH, and the Pattern Group L "speeding up your site" patterns, among others) are named only in passing and not promoted to concept pages, consistent with prior chapters — except [[action-buttons]] and [[search-action-module]], which already have pages from Chapter 2 and are linked here since the same patterns reappear. BROWSABLE CONTENT, MULTIPLE WAYS TO NAVIGATE, and SITE ACCESSIBILITY, also named only in passing here, were later resolved when Pattern Group B was ingested — see [[browsable-content]], [[multiple-ways-to-navigate]], and [[accessibility]].

[[amazon|Amazon.com]] and [[google|Google]] are each named here (virtual/A-B testing) and in Chapter 2's list of sites with durable patterns; combined with a third mention in Chapter 1, both later got minimal entity pages — see [[vanduyne-2006-customer-centered-web-design]]'s notes. Production/implementation tools mentioned here (Dreamweaver, GoLive, Macromedia Director) echo tools named in Chapter 4 but are treated as out-of-scope implementation detail per `purpose.md`, not as entities.

# Glossary

The book's full glossary (A–X) — several hundred short dictionary-style definitions of terms used throughout *The Design of Sites*, most cross-referencing the chapter or pattern where the term is actually explained in full. Functions here as a cross-check against this wiki's existing pages rather than a new source of argument.

## Key points

- The large majority of UX/design-process terms defined here ([[iterative-design]], low-/medium-/high-fidelity and horizontal/vertical prototypes, heuristic evaluation, bottom-line/process data, critical incident, think-aloud protocol, persona, scenario, [[task-analysis]], [[card-sorting]]/affinity diagramming, the seven [[web-site-development-process]] phases, [[design-centering-styles|company-/technology-/designer-/customer-centered design]], gold-plating, brochureware, back-end cost, [[up-front-value-proposition|value proposition]], information scent, Fitts's Law, red-green deficiency) match the definitions already captured in this wiki's concept pages, with no contradictions found — a useful accuracy check on prior ingestion sessions.
- The large majority of the remaining entries are pure Web-technology/implementation terminology (AJAX, CSS, HTML, HTTP/HTTPS, JavaScript, XML, cookies, encryption, image formats, bandwidth/connection types, server/scripting technologies, ad-metric jargon like CPM and click-through) — out of scope per `purpose.md`'s "specific code implementation" exclusion, so none of these were promoted to pages.
- A handful of entries added genuinely new, in-scope nuance not previously captured: **personalization** vs. **customization** as related-but-distinct ideas (system-inferred adaptation vs. customer-chosen configuration), folded into [[homepage-portal]]; and **profile** as a lighter-weight, less-detailed alternative to persona, folded into [[personas-and-scenarios]].
- The glossary names Yahoo! as the best-known example of a **directory** (a hand-curated, hierarchically-organized link listing, as opposed to an algorithmic search engine) — added to [[yahoo]].
- SITE BRANDING (E1) and PRIVACY POLICY (E4) are each defined only in one-sentence stub form here, cross-referencing patterns from the not-yet-ingested Pattern Group E — consistent with the gap already flagged in the 2026-06-24 lint pass; still not enough material for a dedicated page.

## Notes for the wiki

No new entities — the only named real-world things here besides Yahoo! are single, incidental technology/tool mentions (Adobe Illustrator, Macromedia Flash, Apache, Netscape, America Online, Skype, the W3C), each clearly an implementation detail rather than a UX concept, so none were promoted. No new concept pages created either: every genuinely new idea here was either (a) a single-sentence stub that doesn't meet the "explanation beyond a sentence" threshold, or (b) folded as a refinement into an existing page rather than split out on its own, per the "prefer updating existing pages" rule.

# Involving Customers with Iterative Design

Chapter 4 of *The Design of Sites*. Covers the second pillar of [[customer-centered-design]] — keeping customers involved throughout design via [[iterative-design]] — and its three constituent activities: setting goals and designing, [[rapid-prototyping]], and [[usability-evaluation-methods|evaluating]] designs with real customers.

## Key points

- **Why iterate** ([[iterative-design]]): no design team has complete information about customer needs, and errors caught late in a project cost roughly 10x more to fix than if caught early, ballooning to 100–1000x after deployment. A 2x2 "right feature × right implementation" framework explains the distinct kinds of design mistakes iteration helps catch.
- **Design goals and principles**: goals (faster task completion, fewer errors, higher conversion, etc.) come from business/customer analysis ([[know-your-customers]]); seven design principles, adapted from Shneiderman, Nielsen, and Tufte, guide how patterns get applied to reach those goals — covered in depth in [[interface-design-principles]].
- **Rapid prototyping** ([[rapid-prototyping]]): site maps, storyboards, and schematics as early design artifacts; a fidelity spectrum from low-fi paper sketches through medium-fi to high-fi HTML mockups; horizontal (broad, shallow) vs. vertical (narrow, deep) prototypes; explicit advice to avoid computer-based design tools until structural issues are settled.
- **Evaluation** ([[usability-evaluation-methods]]): three complementary techniques — expert/heuristic review, informal qualitative evaluation (think-aloud protocol, critical incidents), and formal quantitative usability studies (bottom-line data) — used in combination across the project lifecycle rather than as alternatives.
- The chapter explicitly frames itself as the second of two pillars of customer-centered design introduced in Chapter 3, closing the loop opened by [[know-your-customers]].

## Notes for the wiki

No entities met the 2+ source threshold here. Named individuals/sources mentioned only incidentally — Frank Lloyd Wright, Watts Humphrey, the Standish Group International, Ben Shneiderman, Jakob Nielsen, Edward Tufte, Mark Newman, Mark Rettig, Carolyn Snyder, and the consulting firm User Interface Engineering — are folded inline into the concept pages as supporting attribution rather than promoted to entity pages. The same applies to the research tool DENIM (mentioned once, in [[rapid-prototyping]]). Several design patterns are named only in passing (e.g., LOCATION BREAD CRUMBS, SHOPPING CART, STYLE SHEETS, NAVIGATION BAR, SECURE CONNECTIONS, PROGRESS BAR, PREVENTING ERRORS, MEANINGFUL ERROR MESSAGES, PREDICTIVE INPUT, ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT, GRID LAYOUT, QUICK-FLOW CHECKOUT, SIGN-IN/NEW ACCOUNT, PERSONALIZED CONTENT) and are not promoted to concept pages, consistent with the approach taken for [[web-design-patterns]]. MULTIPLE WAYS TO NAVIGATE, also named only in passing here, was later resolved when Pattern Group B was ingested — see [[multiple-ways-to-navigate]].

# Knowing Your Customers: Principles and Techniques

Chapter 3 of *The Design of Sites*. Argues that [[customer-centered-design]] rests on two pillars — knowing your customers (this chapter) and keeping them involved iteratively (the book's next chapter) — and that knowing your customers is itself a prerequisite for choosing and customizing design patterns well.

## Key points

- **Principles** ([[know-your-customers]]): design teams are not their customers; every design balances four competing elements — people, tasks, technology, and social/organizational context.
- **Accessibility** ([[accessibility]]): a concrete example (red–green color deficiency defeating a color-only error message) shows why the "people" element includes basic perceptual variation, not just demographics.
- **Task analysis** ([[task-analysis]]): a structured way of cataloging what customers currently do and want to do, illustrated by two failure stories (an unfindable refund form; a dentist's billing system that ignored handwritten margin notes).
- **Personas and scenarios** ([[personas-and-scenarios]]): context-rich stories and recurring fictional customers used to sanity-check design decisions before any software is built, illustrated with a worked "Victoria/ebirthdayz.com" example.
- **Customer research methods** ([[customer-research-methods]]): ethnographic observation, interviews, surveys, and focus groups, each with distinct strengths, costs, and the recurring caveat that surveys and focus groups capture what people *say*, not what they *do*.
- **Card sorting** ([[card-sorting]]): affinity diagramming and card sorting turn raw observation/interview data into a candidate information architecture.

## Notes for the wiki

No entities met the 2+ source threshold here either. The chapter cites several named individuals incidentally (Leysia Palen's 1999 group-calendar study, Douglas Engelbart's "tricycle vs. bicycle" quote, futurist Paul Saffo's "threshold of indignation" quote) and a named HCI law (Fitts's Law) — all mentioned inline in [[know-your-customers]] as supporting evidence rather than promoted to entity pages.

# Making Site Search Fast and Relevant

Pattern Group J of *The Design of Sites* — three patterns (J1-J3) covering on-site search, from the search box itself through to the results page.

## Key points

- [[search-action-module]] (J1) — already a concept page in this wiki from Chapter 2's worked example; this chapter gives it full six-part pattern documentation for the first time, adding the search-selector default/threshold guidance that the earlier worked example already captured. Updated the existing page rather than duplicating it.
- [[straightforward-search-forms]] (J2) — new page. Why Boolean search syntax and exact-match-only engines frustrate customers, and how to compensate (plain-language phrasing, dictionary/synonym handling, defaulting category searches to "all").
- [[organized-search-results]] (J3) — new page. How to make a results page usable: relevant per-domain summaries, grouped/organized hits, real hyperlinked titles, log-file-driven tuning for common terms and misspellings, and support for common search tasks (repeated search box, large pagination targets).

## Notes for the wiki

Two existing entities picked up genuine new substance (not just incidental mentions): [[amazon]] (category-grouped results, graceful misspelling correction) and [[google]] (minimalist results-page design, large accessible pagination target). A new entity page was created for [[ibm]] — it now has two *substantive*, dedicated examples across two sources (the Chapter 1 business case, and this chapter's notebook/laptop/ThinkPad synonym-handling example), clearing the bar that thin repeated mentions alone don't.

[[accessibility]] gained a new facet from this chapter: large clickable targets as a motor-control accessibility consideration, not just the perceptual (color-vision) angle it previously covered. [[card-sorting]] was linked forward to [[organized-search-results]], since the source explicitly recommends using card-sorting vocabulary to anticipate search terms pre-launch.

No other new entities — IMDb, Barnes & Noble, Zagat, and Epicurious are single-use screenshot examples. Epicurious specifically is now a *second* incidental mention (it previously appeared as a screenshot in Pattern Group C), but both mentions are bare screenshots with no explanatory paragraph, so it stays below the entity threshold — flagged for reconsideration if a future source adds real depth, consistent with how CNN's repeated thin mentions were handled in the Pattern Group I session.

Referenced-but-not-yet-explained patterns from this chapter, for future prioritization: CLEAR FORMS (H10), WRITING FOR SEARCH ENGINES (D6), DISTINCTIVE HTML TITLES (D9), and LOCATION-BASED SERVICES (M3). D6 and D9 both belong to Pattern Group D, reinforcing that group's priority (already flagged for PAGE TEMPLATE, D1, in the previous session). BROWSABLE CONTENT (B2), MULTIPLE WAYS TO NAVIGATE (B1), and SITE ACCESSIBILITY (B9), also referenced here, were later resolved when Pattern Group B was ingested — see [[browsable-content]], [[multiple-ways-to-navigate]], and [[accessibility]].

# Speeding Up Your Site

Pattern Group L of *The Design of Sites* — six patterns (L1-L6) for making a site download, and feel, fast: LOW NUMBER OF FILES, FAST-LOADING IMAGES, SEPARATE TABLES, HTML POWER, REUSABLE IMAGES, and FAST-LOADING CONTENT.

## Key points

- L1-L5 are almost entirely implementation technique: removing unnecessary files, image file-format trade-offs (GIF vs. JPEG vs. PNG, cropping/shrinking/compression ratios, progressive-scan vs. interlaced loading), splitting large HTML tables so the top of a page renders before the rest, using native HTML markup instead of images, and relying on browser image caching. Per `purpose.md`'s "specific code implementation" exclusion, none of this was extracted into wiki pages — it sits below the UX/design-principle level this wiki tracks, regardless of how many sources might eventually repeat it.
- L6 (FAST-LOADING CONTENT) is the one pattern in this group with real UX substance: the distinction between actual speed and *perceived* speed, and a taxonomy of progress indicators — a static text message, an animated "marching ants" indicator, a thermometer, and progressively displaying content as it arrives — each with a real trade-off (a thermometer is the most reassuring when accurate, but worse than no indicator at all when it isn't). This is a direct, concrete elaboration of the "offer informative feedback" principle already documented in [[interface-design-principles]], so it was folded into that page's existing feedback principle rather than split into a new one.
- [[homepage-portal]] already noted that "fast downloads matter as much as content" — added a forward link there to [[interface-design-principles]]'s feedback principle, for the case where a homepage genuinely can't be made fast enough and the next-best thing is managing the wait itself.

## Notes for the wiki

No new concept or entity pages. All of this chapter's screenshot examples (Yahoo!, Geocaching.com, Craigslist, Napster, Lowestfare.com, PETA, BabyCenter, the Lincoln Highway site, SideStep, A9.com, Yahoo! Mail, Expedia, Zazzle, Kayak.com, Google Maps) are single-source and illustrate implementation techniques rather than UX substance — and for several (image formats, HTML attributes, AJAX), the subject itself is out of scope regardless of source count, so they wouldn't earn a page even with repetition elsewhere. Yahoo!'s minimalist-homepage example here simply reconfirms what [[yahoo]] already says about its fast, sparse-graphics interface — no new substance was added to that page.

# Making the Most of Web Design Patterns

Chapter 2 of *The Design of Sites*. Explains the book's design-pattern framework ([[web-design-patterns]]): what a pattern is, the six-part format used to document each pattern (name, background, problem, forces, solution, other patterns to consider), how patterns connect into a larger pattern language, and how patterns are grouped (A–M) to support different stages of the design process.

## Key points

- Patterns are traced back to architect Christopher Alexander's *A Pattern Language: Towns, Buildings, Construction* (1977), which proposed a shared language for design decisions. The authors adapt this idea to Web design, with an explicit emphasis on customer-centered design ([[customer-centered-design]]).
- A worked example, [[action-buttons]], demonstrates the full pattern format and the idea of "forces" — the competing concerns (visibility, size, download speed) a designer must balance.
- A second worked example, [[search-action-module]], shows the format applied to a more complex pattern with a background, search-selector trade-offs, and links to related patterns.
- A running example follows a designer, Sarah, using the pattern language plus customer testing (recruiting five participants, observing a checkout flow) to diagnose and redesign a broken checkout — illustrating [[process-funnel]] and [[customer-centered-design]] in practice.
- The chapter argues that core interaction patterns (navigation bars, search boxes, checkout flows) have stayed stable across major sites ([[ebay]], [[amazon]], [[google]], [[yahoo]], CNN, MSN) from 2001–2006 even as visual styling evolved, supporting the claim that the underlying patterns are durable.

## Notes for the wiki

At the time this source was first ingested (2026-06-23), no entities met the 2+ source threshold — Christopher Alexander, eBay, and Amazon were all mentioned only incidentally in this single chapter. That has since changed: [[ebay]], [[amazon]], [[google]], and [[yahoo]] each picked up enough additional incidental mentions across later-ingested sources (Chapters 1 and 5) to cross the threshold, and all four now have entity pages — see [[vanduyne-2006-customer-centered-web-design]]'s notes for that reversal. Christopher Alexander remains a single-source incidental mention. Most of the ~30 named patterns referenced in this chapter (e.g., PROCESS FUNNEL, ACTION BUTTONS, SEARCH ACTION MODULE) are only briefly named; only the patterns that received a full explanation in this chapter were promoted to concept pages.

# Affordance (Wikipedia)

An encyclopedia article on affordance: the psychology term for what an environment offers an actor, its 1988 narrowing for design/HCI use by Don Norman, and the surrounding terminology (signifiers, perceived/hidden/false affordances). Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-25), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- James J. Gibson coined "affordance" in his 1966 book *The Senses Considered as Perceptual Systems*; his most-cited definition is from *The Ecological Approach to Visual Perception* (1979): "the affordances of the environment are what it offers the animal, what it provides or furnishes, either for good or ill." Gibson's affordances are relational (depend on the actor's current capabilities) and objective — they exist whether or not the actor perceives them, e.g. a chair objectively affords throwing as well as sitting.
- In 1988, Donald Norman narrowed the term in an HCI context to only those action possibilities an actor can *readily perceive* — popularized via *The Design of Everyday Things* and now the dominant usage in design/HCI, distinct from (and often conflated with) Gibson's broader original sense.
- Norman later judged this narrowing unintended; his 2013 revision of the same book introduced **signifiers** to separate the two jobs: "Affordances determine what actions are possible. Signifiers communicate where the action should take place. We need both."
- William Gaver's three-way split: **perceivable** affordances (apparent to the actor), **hidden** affordances (real, but not apparent — e.g. a shoe can pry a cork from a wine bottle, but this isn't visible from looking at a shoe), and **false** affordances (apparent, but with no real function — e.g. a placebo button).
- Worked examples: a door knob's shape as a perceivable affordance of how it's used; tea cups/teapots shaped to suggest their function; graphical user interfaces, where affordance is named as one of several design principles.
- The term has drifted toward an uncountable noun describing ease of discoverability generally ("this button has good affordance"), with "afford" coming to mean "suggest" or "invite" — a usage shift the article flags as inconsistent even across HCI textbooks.
- Jenny L. Davis's 2016/2020 "mechanisms and conditions" framework reframes the question from "what does a technology afford" to "how does it afford, for whom, under what circumstances" — technologies can request, demand, encourage, discourage, refuse, or allow an action depending on a user's perception, dexterity, and cultural/institutional standing.
- Cross-disciplinary use noted beyond design/HCI: criminology, industrial design, instructional design, STS, sports science, AI, and fire-safety signage/evacuation design.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-affordance.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0 (confirmed in the HTML footer), same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

Created a new concept page, [[affordance]] — the term had been used informally on [[action-buttons]] ("this works as a visual affordance") without ever being explained; this article supplies the underlying theory (Gibson vs. Norman, signifiers, the perceivable/hidden/false split) that the existing pattern page was implicitly leaning on.

**What was left out, and why** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): the language-education extensions (Van Lier, Rietveld & Kiverstein, Nguyen) — pedagogy/applied-linguistics theory, not UI/UX; the criminology, sports-science, and AI cross-disciplinary applications — named only in passing, with no design-specific content to extract; the full citation/footnote apparatus.

# Card Sorting (Wikipedia)

Wikipedia's article on card sorting, a knowledge-elicitation method for understanding how people mentally categorize information, used to design information architecture, navigation, and menu structure.

## Key points

- **Basic method.** A representative participant receives index cards bearing item names, groups them in whatever way seems logical, and names each group; this repeats across several participants, and the results are analyzed afterward for patterns (a category tree, or "dendrogram").
- **Open card sorting** (generative): participants invent their own category names — reveals both how people classify items and what words they use for the categories, useful for discovering a new organizational scheme.
- **Closed card sorting** (evaluative): participants sort into a fixed, predetermined set of category names — reveals how much agreement there is on what belongs where, useful for judging an existing or proposed scheme rather than generating one.
- **Reverse card sorting**, more commonly called **tree testing**: tests an existing category hierarchy in isolation. Participants are given a task and have to find the right place for it by navigating the card-based hierarchy from the top down, with none of a real site's navigational aids (search, breadcrumbs) — isolates whether the structure itself, not the UI around it, supports the task.
- **Modified-Delphi card sorting** (created by Celeste Paul): only the first participant does a full independent sort; each subsequent participant iterates on the previous participant's result rather than starting over. Builds consensus with fewer participants than independent sorting.
- **Analysis** falls into two families: **semantic** methods examine the labels/descriptions participants gave (e.g. *gist analysis*, grouping differently-worded labels that mean the same thing — "Difficult to understand" and "Confusing"); **syntactic** methods examine the structure of the sorts themselves — card-based analysis builds similarity/dissimilarity matrices of which cards landed together, feeding cluster analysis and dendrograms; sort-based analysis measures the "edit distance" (card moves needed to turn one participant's sort into another's, calculable via the Hungarian algorithm) between whole sorts.
- **Online/remote card sorting**: web-based tools reach more participants at lower cost and can automate analysis, but lose the moderator's ability to ask a participant *why* they grouped something a certain way.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-card-sorting.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

This wiki already has a concept page for this exact topic, [[card-sorting]] (paired with affinity diagramming), so rather than create a duplicate, folded this article's new material directly into it: the open/closed distinction, reverse card sorting (tree testing), Modified-Delphi card sorting, a brief note on semantic vs. syntactic analysis, and the remote/online tradeoff.

**Excluded:** the deeper statistical mechanics of syntactic analysis (the Hungarian algorithm's actual computation, orthogonality's minimum-spanning-tree formula, multidimensional scaling) — named so a reader recognizes the terms, but not derived, since the math itself is outside `purpose.md`'s design-practice scope; the "See also" pointers to cluster analysis, group concept mapping, and Q methodology as general statistical/research techniques rather than card-sorting-specific content; and citation-level bibliographic detail (e.g. the specific Nielsen and Maurer/Warfel references), beyond crediting Celeste Paul by name for the one technique she's specifically credited with originating.

# Color Theory (Wikipedia)

An encyclopedia article on color theory: the historical, largely artistic body of knowledge about color mixing, contrast, harmony, schemes, and symbolism, distinguished in the article from the more objective discipline of color science. Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-25), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- Defines color theory as covering color mixing, contrast effects, harmony, schemes, and symbolism — subjective/artistic, as opposed to color science's objective/functional focus (chemistry, astronomy, color reproduction).
- Traces a long history (Aristotle, Bharata's *Nāṭya Shāstra*, several medieval Islamic and European scholars, Newton, Goethe's 1810 *Theory of Colours*, Chevreul's 1839 Law of Simultaneous Color Contrast, Young–Helmholtz trichromatic theory, Munsell's 1905 system) — named but not elaborated on beyond a sentence each.
- **Color mixing**: additive (RGB, light-based — screens) vs. subtractive (CMYK, ink/pigment-based — print); CMYK is economical but structurally weak at reproducing orange and some purples.
- **Color schemes**: complementary (opposite hues, cancel to achromatic, strong contrast), analogous (adjacent hues, monochromatic feel), split-complementary (a softened complementary pair), and triadic (three equidistant hues) — all derived from the color wheel.
- **Color contrast** (Chevreul's three types): simultaneous (side-by-side hues alter each other's apparent color), successive (afterimage on a neutral background), mixed (afterimage cast onto a different color).
- **Warm vs. cool**: a centuries-old distinction (warm = red-yellow, associated with daylight/advancing; cool = blue-green-violet, associated with overcast/receding), with the caveat that the psychological effect may really be driven by warm pigments' typically higher saturation and lighter value, not hue itself.
- **Color harmony**: a 21st-century model treats it as a function of the colors involved *and* individual differences, cultural experience, context, perceptual effects, and time-bound trends — and notes that with ~2.3 million perceivable colors, the combination space is effectively infinite, making any fixed predictive harmony formula "fundamentally unsound."
- **Color symbolism**: culture- and context-bound, not universal or intrinsic (red alone connotes excitement, danger, luck, romance, femininity depending on context) — and color's claimed therapeutic properties have no real evidential support.
- Brief, generic mentions of camouflage, heat-dispersing paint, and theme-park aesthetics (Disney) as "functional applications" — one sentence, no design-specific elaboration.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-color-theory.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0 (confirmed in the HTML footer), same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

Created concept page [[color-theory]] — color schemes, contrast, and the harmony/symbolism caveats clear the "explanation beyond a sentence" bar easily, and nothing existing in the wiki covered color models, schemes, or the culture-bound-symbolism point. Linked forward from [[clear-first-reads]]'s existing "complementary colors" phrase (previously unexplained) and extended [[accessibility]]'s "never rely on color alone" guidance with a second, independent reason: color *meaning* isn't universal even for customers with typical color vision.

**What was left out, and why** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): the full history of named theorists (Aristotle through the Bauhaus figures — Kandinsky, Itten, Birren, Albers) — named but not elaborated, consistent with the existing precedent for bare-practitioner lists; RYB/Munsell color-system technical detail beyond the RGB/CMYK distinction actually useful for screen-vs-print design; the split-primary-palette theory (a painting-pigment-mixing technique, not a UI concern); the tints-and-shades section (same scope exclusion); camouflage, heat-dispersing paint, and theme-park design as one-sentence, non-elaborated "functional application" examples, none of which are UI/UX. No new entities — the named historical and Bauhaus figures stay unelaborated practitioners, consistent with the precedent set for Mayhew/Norman/Cooper/Shneiderman/Tufte/Whiteside/Bennett.

**Media:** the article contains nine figures, all hosted on Wikimedia Commons (not embedded in the local HTML snapshot): Goethe's 1810 color wheel, Hayter's 1826 diagram, Munsell's 1905 system diagram, additive color mixing (`AdditiveColor.svg`) and subtractive color mixing (`SubtractiveColor.svg`) — the most directly UI-relevant pair, illustrating the RGB/CMYK distinction — the RYB color star (primary/secondary/tertiary), Chevreul's 1855 chromatic diagram, and two historical color-system plates (Lichtenberg 1775; Schiffermüller 1772). No `raw/media/` subfolder created, consistent with all prior Wikipedia ingestions.

# The Design of Everyday Things (Wikipedia)

An encyclopedia article on Donald Norman's 1988 book — the work that popularized "affordance" for design (already covered via [[affordance]]) and the source of the seven-stages-of-action / Gulf of Execution / Gulf of Evaluation framework, not yet documented in this wiki before now. Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-25), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- Originally published 1988 as *The Psychology of Everyday Things* (POET/DOET); a new preface was added in 2002, and a revised, expanded edition followed in 2013 (the edition that added **signifiers**). Norman has said his academic peers preferred the original title, but he judged the new one better at communicating the book's content and attracting readers.
- The book's origin story: Norman, at an Italian conference, watched several people fail to thread a film projector despite trying one after another, prompting him to study why ordinary objects are so often hard to use.
- **Affordances and signifiers**: Norman borrowed "affordance" from James J. Gibson; the book's worked example is doors that can be pushed or pulled, with signifiers (flat plates, small push-buttons, long rounded bars) communicating which. Norman discusses door handles at length — already covered in depth via [[affordance]], so not re-extracted here beyond confirming the book as the original source.
- **User-centered design**: Norman popularized this term (used earlier in his 1986 book *User-Centered System Design*) to mean designing around user needs ahead of secondary concerns like aesthetics — operationalized as simplifying task structure, making things visible, getting mapping right, exploiting constraints, designing for error, explaining affordances, and the seven stages of action.
- **Seven stages of action**: four execution stages (forming the goal → forming the intention → specifying an action → executing it) and three evaluation stages (perceiving the world's state → interpreting it → evaluating against intentions/goals) — see [[seven-stages-of-action]] for the full model, including the **Gulf of Execution** and **Gulf of Evaluation** it produces, and the four design principles (visibility, a good conceptual model, good mappings, feedback) it distills down to.
- **Reception**: an early draft offended a group of industrial designers, prompting Norman to rewrite it more sympathetically toward the profession — the only reception detail the article gives.
- "See also" lists **Action slip** (the same slip/mistake distinction this wiki already documents via [[usability-engineering]], not separately extracted), *Emotional Design* (a different Norman book), Industrial design, Interaction design, and Principles of user interface design (already covered via [[interface-design-principles]]) — all bare pointers, not elaborated on in this article.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-design-of-everyday-things.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0 (confirmed in the HTML footer), same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted, aside from the one short illustrative quote on the gulf of execution that's directly attributed.

Created one new concept page, [[seven-stages-of-action]] — the action-cycle model, both gulfs, and the VCR/lamp/projector examples clear the "explanation beyond a sentence" bar easily. The article's own "four main principles" distillation (visibility, conceptual model, mapping, feedback) gets only one sentence each here, below that same bar individually, so they're folded into [[seven-stages-of-action]] as a closing summary rather than split into separate pages; feedback is cross-linked to [[interface-design-principles]]'s existing, more developed treatment of the same idea.

Added this as a second source to [[affordance]] (confirming, not changing, its existing Gibson/Norman/signifiers content) and a historical footnote to [[customer-centered-design]] (Norman's "user-centered design" as the term this wiki's "customer-centered design" deliberately varies from).

**What was left out, and why** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): the book's other named chapter topics (The Psychopathology of Everyday Things, Knowledge in the Head and in the World, Knowing What to Do, To Err Is Human, The Design Challenge) are listed by title only in the article, with no elaborating content to extract; the Reception section's title-change anecdote, folded into this page's notes rather than given a page of its own (too thin, and not a design concept); the bare "See also" pointers, per the established precedent of not forcing pages onto unelaborated names. No new entities — Norman, like Mayhew, Cooper, Shneiderman, Tufte, Whiteside, and Bennett before him, stays a named-but-unelaborated practitioner rather than a dedicated entity page, consistent with that precedent; the book itself isn't a separate entity either, since its content is already fully represented through this source page and the concept pages it feeds.

# Fitts's Law (Wikipedia)

A dedicated encyclopedia article on Fitts's Law — previously only a one-sentence, below-threshold mention in this wiki (from [[vanduyne-2006-knowing-your-customers]] and the book's glossary), now substantial enough to justify its own concept page, [[fitts-law]]. Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-24), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- Paul Fitts published the original 1954 study in the *Journal of Experimental Psychology*, proposing a way to quantify how hard a target-selection task is.
- Movement time depends on distance to the target and the target's width, following MT = a + b·log₂(2D/W) — the log₂(2D/W) term is the **index of difficulty**.
- Design implications well beyond "make buttons bigger": the rule of the infinite edges (a target flush against a screen edge is effectively infinite in size along that edge — why macOS puts its menu bar at the very top), "magic corners" (where two infinite edges meet, exploited by Windows/Office for high-value controls), touchscreen target sizing, and handedness effects in radial/pie menus.
- Related laws: Hick's Law (decision time vs. number of choices), the Accot–Zhai steering law (extending Fitts's Law to constrained-path navigation like hierarchical menus), and throughput (bits/second, combining difficulty and time).
- Documented limitations: predictive accuracy degrades when distance and width both vary widely in the same comparison; the 1D formulation needs adjustment for real 2D pointing; applying it to eye-tracking/gaze interaction is contested, since saccadic eye movement isn't the same kind of motor act as a deliberate hand movement.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-fittss-law.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

**What was left out, and why** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): the full mathematical derivation and the Shannon-formulation history behind the index-of-difficulty formula were excluded as more information-theory than UX design — [[fitts-law]] keeps the formula but not its derivation. Specific numeric studies/citations validating or critiquing the law in particular populations (cited in the article's body but not summarized above) were also left out as academic-literature detail rather than a design-relevant fact. Everything else from the `WebFetch` summary was used; nothing was excluded for being out of `purpose.md`'s scope outright, since this entire article is squarely a design heuristic.

This is another case of a "mentioned but below threshold" topic getting reconsidered once a dedicated source appeared — same pattern as the [[wcag]]/[[wai-aria]] splits. Updated [[know-your-customers]], [[accessibility]], [[organized-search-results]], and [[action-buttons]] to link the new page where each already discussed the underlying idea without naming it.

Reclassified [[fitts-law]] from `entity` to `concept` (2026-06-25) after `schema.md` was updated to classify laws as concepts rather than entities.

# Gestalt Psychology (Wikipedia)

An encyclopedia article on Gestalt psychology, the perceptual-organization school behind several layout principles already used (without being named) elsewhere in this wiki. Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-24), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- Founded by Max Wertheimer, Wolfgang Köhler, and Kurt Koffka in the early 20th century; Wertheimer's 1912 paper on the phi phenomenon marks the formal start. Core claim: "the whole is something else than the sum of its parts."
- Eight named principles of perceptual organization: proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, common fate, symmetry, figure-ground organization, and the law of past experience.
- Explicitly names interface design (radio-button placement) as a field these principles apply to, alongside cartography and desktop layout design; cites the IBM logo as a closure example and a Mississippi River map as a combined similarity/closure/proximity/continuity example.
- Documented criticism: the theory is more descriptive than explanatory — it reliably names perceptual effects without a precise, settled mechanism for why they occur, and some textbooks call the "laws" vague as stated.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-gestalt-psychology.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

Created a new concept page, [[gestalt-psychology]], rather than an entity page — unlike [[fitts-law]] and [[hicks-law]] (single quantifiable relationships), this is a broader framework with multiple named sub-principles, closer in kind to existing framework-type concept pages like [[web-design-patterns]]. The single-source threshold for concepts ("explanation beyond a sentence") is clearly met; concepts don't carry the entity rule's 2+-source requirement.

**What was left out, and why** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): the Gestalt school's broader history (its influence on social psychology, the Nazi-era emigration of its founders) — outside `purpose.md`'s UI/UX scope. The article's own cartography example was only summarized in passing, since [[grid-layout]] and [[clear-first-reads]] already supply this wiki's own Web-design illustrations of the same principles.

# Hick's Law (Wikipedia)

A dedicated encyclopedia article on Hick's Law — previously only a one-sentence mention on [[fitts-law]] ("Related laws"), now substantial enough to justify its own concept page, [[hicks-law]]. Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-24), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- William Edmund Hick published the original 1952 study; Ray Hyman's 1953 work is closely related, so the law is sometimes called the Hick–Hyman law.
- Decision time grows logarithmically with the number of choices: T = b·log₂(n+1) for equally-probable choices. Doubling the options does not double the time.
- The logarithmic benefit depends on the choice set being *organized* — an alphabetized or categorized menu lets people subdivide it efficiently; an unstructured list of the same length forces linear scanning, and the article states plainly that the law "does not apply" in that case.
- Documented limitations: doesn't hold for verbal responses to very familiar stimuli, can show no effect or a reversed effect for reflexive eye-movement (saccade) responses, and unequal-probability choice sets need an information-theoretic (entropy-based) adjustment to the formula.
- E. Roth (1964) found a correlation between an individual's Hick's-Law processing-speed slope and IQ.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-hicks-law.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

**What was left out, and why** (per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement): the entropy-based formula for unequal-probability choice sets is named but not derived in [[hicks-law]] — the derivation is more information theory than UX design. Nothing was excluded for being outside `purpose.md`'s scope outright; like [[wikipedia-2026-fittss-law]], this entire article is a design-relevant heuristic.

Linked the new page from [[homepage-portal]] (the 95%/5% above-the-fold rule is a concrete instance of progressive disclosure reducing decision load) and from [[fitts-law]] (upgraded the existing one-line "related law" mention to a full cross-reference now that both have their own pages).

Reclassified [[hicks-law]] from `entity` to `concept` (2026-06-25) after `schema.md` was updated to classify laws as concepts rather than entities.

# Stroop Effect (Wikipedia)

An encyclopedia article on the Stroop effect: the measurable delay in reaction time when people are asked to name the ink color of a color word printed in a conflicting color (e.g. naming the color of the word "red" printed in blue ink). John Ridley Stroop published the foundational 1935 study; the effect had been independently documented by Erich Jaensch in Germany in 1929. Wikipedia articles are continuously edited, so `year` above reflects the access date (2026-06-24), not a fixed publication year.

## Key points

- The leading explanation (**automaticity theory**) is that reading is so practiced it happens automatically and consumes attentional resources that color-naming then has to compete for; three other explanations (processing-speed theory, selective-attention theory, parallel distributed processing) are also documented but less widely accepted.
- The classic task uses three stimulus types: color names printed in black, color names printed in an incongruent color, and plain colored squares — naming ink color is measurably slower and more error-prone for the incongruent condition than for the neutral ones.
- An **emotional Stroop** variant shows the same kind of interference happens with emotionally-loaded words, not just color words — suggesting the underlying mechanism is about competing automatic/salient associations generally, not color specifically.
- The article documents mostly clinical/diagnostic applications (measuring selective attention and processing speed, screening for ADHD and depression) rather than interface-design applications — it does **not** discuss icon/label mismatches, UI color conventions, or any other direct design case study.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-stroop-effect.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

**No new entity page was created.** Unlike [[wikipedia-2026-fittss-law]] and [[wikipedia-2026-hicks-law]], this is the *only* source in this wiki that mentions the Stroop effect — it doesn't clear `schema.md`'s explicit "2+ sources" entity threshold, regardless of how substantial this one article is. It's also thinner on direct design application than either law's source was; the article itself says plainly that it documents clinical uses, not interface-design ones. Parking this here rather than creating a page: if a second source ever discusses the Stroop effect with a real design connection, promote it then.

**What was extracted, and what was left out, per `schema.md`'s ingestion-report requirement:** folded one sentence into [[accessibility]]'s existing "never rely on color alone" point — the automaticity-theory explanation for *why* conflicting color/text cues create friction, not just *that* they do. Left out: the four competing theoretical explanations beyond automaticity (academic psychology detail beyond what a one-line design grounding needs), the classic experimental task's full methodology, the emotional Stroop variant, and the clinical/diagnostic applications (ADHD/depression screening) — all out of `purpose.md`'s UI/UX design scope.

# Usability Engineering (Wikipedia)

Wikipedia's article on usability engineering, the professional discipline that studies and tests the usability of interactive systems, distinct from (though closely allied with) UX and interaction design.

## Key points

- **Definition.** Usability engineering draws on computer science and psychology to identify usability problems, rather than to design solutions for them — a usability engineer's focus is the research/testing phase; finding the actual fix is the job of a UX or interaction designer.
- **History.** In 1988, John Whiteside (Digital Equipment Corporation) and John Bennett (IBM) published work isolating three key activities: early goal-setting, iterative evaluation, and prototyping. Jakob Nielsen's 1993 book *Usability Engineering* became the field's defining text, describing methods for testing throughout a product's development — checking for barriers to **learnability, efficiency, memorability, error-free use, and subjective satisfaction** before launch — and coining the term "usability engineering lifecycle."
- **International standards.** ISO 9241 part 11 defines usability operationally as the context, efficiency, and satisfaction with which specified users can perform tasks. NIST's Common Industry Specification for Usability and biometrics usability specs apply the same idea in U.S. industry contexts. (The article's accessibility-standards bullet list — WAI, Section 508, ADA, IBM's guidelines — duplicates ground already covered in this wiki's [[accessibility]] and [[wai]] pages.)
- **Errors: slips vs. mistakes.** A two-part taxonomy of human error: a **slip** is the right goal executed via the wrong action (a typo, clicking the wrong menu item — an automatic-behavior failure); a **mistake** is a consciously deliberated but wrong goal in the first place. A **mode error** is a dangerous slip subtype where a user acts correctly for the mode they think they're in but is actually in a different one (e.g. a car in reverse instead of drive) — designers mitigate this with modeless interfaces or by requiring a continuous action to stay in a special mode (e.g. holding a key for Photoshop's lasso tool).
- **Evaluation methods.** Lists card sorting, cognitive task analysis, cognitive walkthroughs, contextual inquiry, focus groups, heuristic evaluation, interviews, questionnaires, surveys, think-aloud protocol, and usability testing — all already covered in this wiki ([[customer-research-methods]], [[usability-evaluation-methods]], [[card-sorting]]) — plus one not yet mentioned: the **RITE method** (Rapid Iterative Testing and Evaluation), which fixes problems and reruns tests within the same study rather than waiting for a full round to finish.
- **Notable practitioners:** Deborah Mayhew, Donald Norman, Alan Cooper, Jakob Nielsen, John M. Carroll, Larry Constantine, Mary Beth Rosson, Steve Krug — named but not elaborated on in the article.

## Notes for the wiki

A local snapshot is saved at `raw/safe/wikipedia-usability-engineering.html` alongside the live URL. Licensed CC BY-SA 4.0, same treatment as this wiki's other Wikipedia sources — content folded in is paraphrased, not quoted.

Created concept page [[usability-engineering]] to hold the discipline-level framing (research vs. design-solution focus), the Whiteside & Bennett / Nielsen history, the five usability attributes, the ISO 9241 definition, and the slips/mistakes error taxonomy — content not previously in the wiki under any existing page. Linked it from [[iterative-design]] (naming the 1988 origin of its already-documented design→prototype→evaluate cycle), [[usability-evaluation-methods]] (the five attributes as what evaluation is testing *for*, plus the RITE method as an added technique), and [[interface-design-principles]] (slips vs. mistakes, extending principle 4's error-recovery guidance).

**Excluded:** the bare list of notable practitioners (names without elaboration — out of `purpose.md` scope, which excludes "specific names like authors, editors" absent further development); the "Software applications and development tools" section, covering specific named (mostly defunct) U.S. government tools (Web Metrics Tool Suite, WebSAT/WebCAT/WebVIP/FLUD, the Usability Testing Environment, UsableNet Liftmachine) — implementation-level software products, not design principles; NIST's biometrics-specific usability specs, too narrow for this wiki's scope; and the accessibility-standards bullet list (WAI, Section 508, ADA, IBM guidelines), already covered by [[accessibility]] and [[wai]].
